account.bio:
Uncensored, private, decentralized AI inference network.

CA: EmcxFTNVDqyLHp11NvwvLZ4D7LKGbG9i7B8RF7dwpump

followerSpikes.note: Inferred (no time-series provided): plausible growth driven by Virtuals ecosystem announcement and viral sharded-inference demo threads (RTs of @leyten with 76-184 reposts). Not a confirmed bot spike.

followerQuality.notes: Mixed follower base. A few mid-sized real accounts (@wardogblazeify ~9.3k, @TruthMaximalist ~1.9k, @yukinthecut ~1.5k) but a large share are very low-follower accounts (single digits to low hundreds) with crypto-airdrop/'1000XGEM'/ambassador-style handles — typical of a pump.fun token attracting retail degens. Not conclusively purchased, but quality is diluted. Independent analytics rate the account 'Noted' (~2K total, ~83 top followers) and dev leyten 'Noted' (~5K total, ~123 top followers).

website.design: Polished, custom-built landing page (not a generic template) with a coherent dark theme, pixel-style icons, scroll-driven animation, and clear benefit-oriented messaging (People-Powered AI, Private by Design, In-Browser, Get Paid). Multiple functioning sub-pages (chat, create, earn, staking, treasury, data, worker login) indicate a real working app rather than a static brochure. Minor deduction for some repeated copy blocks in the page source. ~78/100.
website.documentation: Strongest dimension (~82/100). A full Docusaurus-based docs site with substantial technical depth, not marketing fluff. Documents a coherent architecture (user client, orchestrator, workers), concrete engineering decisions, real stack details (WebLLM/WebGPU browser workers, Node.js + ollama native workers, Socket.io orchestrator, ComfyUI image workers), a job lifecycle, weighted-random worker selection, service tiers, model names, and an API reference. Explicit tokenomics (70% worker / 30% margin to treasury, daily 50% buyback&burn / 50% staker USDC rewards). No standalone formal whitepaper PDF, but docs effectively serve that role. A product-update blog also exists.
website.roadmap: Weak (~35/100). No dedicated forward-looking roadmap with milestones, dates, or phased deliverables. The only related signal is a blog announcing already-shipped features (open coding agent, auto-compounding staking, image generation/API). Active shipping is a positive credibility signal, but the absence of any stated future direction or timeline is a meaningful gap.
website.teamInfo: Poor (~20/100). No named, credible team disclosed anywhere on the site or docs — no founders, bios, LinkedIn profiles, or company/legal entity. The only identity breadcrumb is the GitHub username 'leyten' in a footer repo link. For a platform custodying user funds (USDC credits, staking, treasury buyback/burn), an effectively anonymous team is a notable warning sign and limits accountability.
website.notes: Meaningfully more substantive than a typical 'slick site, no substance' launch: pairs a polished custom landing page with genuinely detailed technical docs describing a coherent decentralized-inference architecture and internally-consistent tokenomics, and appears to be a live product with functioning sub-pages and ongoing feature shipping. Two red flags temper the score: (1) no public/named team — effectively anonymous aside from a GitHub handle, concerning given it custodies credits, staking, and a treasury; and (2) no real roadmap or stated milestones. A GitHub link exists but the repo could not be accessed/verified, so 'open-source' claims and actual code activity remain unconfirmed. Tokenomics are heavily buyback/burn-and-staking oriented, warranting normal skepticism about sustainability. Due diligence: verify repo contents/commit activity, confirm staking/treasury contracts on-chain, and seek team-identity evidence before trusting funds.

github.activity: Early-stage and low-volume but recently active — approximately 16 total commits on master, with the repo updated within days of this assessment. Work is organized into phases (Phase 0 complete: two-node split inference with per-node KV-cache; Phases 1-2 for WAN transport and speculative decoding still pending). Actively developed, not abandoned, but immature.
github.relevance: Strong. Not a fork and 100% Python, consistent with a genuine PyTorch/transformers inference engine. The code implements the pipeline-parallel split-inference concept that its associated c0mpute token project markets: layers split into contiguous per-GPU blocks with activations streamed in order and per-node KV-cache. Ships runnable entry points (phase0/node_kv.py, phase0/bench.py), CLI docs, ARCHITECTURE.md and ROADMAP.md, and is candid about its unsolved privacy leak. Genuine engineering, not vaporware.
github.notes: A real, relevant, recently active repo tied to the live c0mpute decentralized inference token project. It genuinely implements the split-inference concept it claims, with a specific and unusually candid technical write-up (openly flags the activation-privacy leak). Weaknesses are maturity and validation: only ~16 commits, 1 star, 0 forks, a single pseudonymous contributor, and self-reported unaudited benchmarks. Mid-range score reflects strong relevance/authenticity offset by minimal traction, bus-factor-of-one, and unproven early-phase work. Caveat: GitHub blocked direct access to commit-history/contributor pages, so cadence and contributor count are inferred from summary metrics.

developers.name: leyten
signals: Owns leyten/shard — open-source pipeline-parallel LLM inference engine (~242 stars) explicitly described as 'the inference engine for c0mpute'
Reproducible benchmarks: 744B model at ~30 tok/s across 7 prosumer Blackwell GPUs in 6 US states over WAN; verifiable receipts (GPU UUIDs, IPs, regions, token hashes) and a PROOF.md
Technical voice in nearly every demo thread; credited by name in mainstream crypto press for the Virtuals integration
Candid about limitations — openly flags activation-leakage to malicious nodes as the #1 open problem rather than overpromising (credibility positive)
developers.qualityNote: Primary builder and strongest positive signal. Pseudonymous but identifiable, with real, inspectable, benchmark-verified code. High credibility for an early-stage project.

signals: Credited as author of an explainer video on the sharding protocol
Likely community/content contributor rather than core engineer

developers.name: Virtuals Protocol
signals: Integration partner, not a developer — integrated leyten's shard engine into the Virtuals agent ecosystem
Partnership corroborated by independent crypto press
developers.qualityNote: Key ecosystem backer/partner; the project's strongest high-signal relationship.

developers.name: leyten
signals: Repo owner and apparently the sole committer on shard
Author of the c0mpute-related inference code
Maintains 13 repositories but minimal social footprint (0 followers, 1 following)
Prior public work is mostly JavaScript/TypeScript (e.g. solcord); shard is a more advanced Python/ML effort relative to that history
developers.qualityNote: Competent, non-trivial Python ML work — pipeline-parallel split inference, custom TCP transport, per-node KV-cache, and a speculative-decoding prototype — described with refreshing honesty about latency and privacy limitations. However it is a one-person, ~16-commit project with no external contributors or independent validation, concentrating all key-person risk on a single pseudonymous account.

engagement.cadence: Active and consistent; mix of original posts and RTs of the lead dev's technical demos. Rising momentum around the Virtuals integration and live benchmark demos.
engagement.notes: Original (non-RT) posts pull solid organic-looking engagement for a 4.7k account: 264-401 likes with 60-87 replies and similar reposts on top tweets. Like-to-reply balance is healthy (not bot-like), and replies contain genuine technical Q&A (answering @snapsnocaps on model-quality limits, @feikuu on the home-GPU vision). Dev demo RTs drew 51-184 retweets. Engagement appears real rather than inflated.

technicalDepth.notes: Standout strength. Real, working, independently inspectable product: open-source shard inference engine with reproducible WAN benchmarks (744B frontier model served at ~30 tok/s across 7 prosumer GPUs in 6 US states). Coordinator design uses speculative decoding (small GLM-4-9B draft proposes tokens, distributed 744B verifies). Live CLI product and USDC credit pricing ($0.01/credit) already documented at docs.c0mpute.ai. Architecture fits academically validated pipeline/layer-wise decentralized inference — grounded, not fantastical. Verifiable receipts and a PROOF.md raise the bar above typical launches.

redFlags.message: Follower base is heavily diluted with very low-follower retail/airdrop-style accounts, typical of a pump.fun launch. Not confirmed as purchased, but quality is low and should be monitored.
A separate c0mpute.com domain exists with similar messaging; unclear whether it's an official alt-domain or a lookalike. The verified bio domain is c0mpute.ai — verify before trusting the .com.

The privacy model is openly incomplete: intermediate activations can leak a fraction of user tokens to a malicious node. The team discloses this honestly as the #1 open problem — a genuine risk to track, but transparency is a credibility positive.

summary: c0mpute is an unusually substance-heavy early-stage project: a pseudonymous-but-identifiable builder (@leyten) ships an open-source, benchmark-verified, pipeline-parallel inference engine ('shard') that already runs a 744B frontier model across consumer/prosumer GPUs over WAN, and it has landed a credible integration with Virtuals Protocol. Engagement looks organic, technical depth is high, and the team candidly discloses the unsolved privacy/activation-leakage problem rather than overpromising. Main risks are typical for the stage — a pump.fun token, diluted retail follower quality, a possible lookalike .com domain, and unproven token-economics at scale — but on the core question of 'is this real?', the evidence strongly says yes.
